does: This paper introduces DataFilter, a test-time defense mechanism that operates at the data layer to protect LLM agents from prompt injection attacks. Rather than modifying the model itself or relying on detection, DataFilter uses a separately trained model to filter malicious instructions from external data before it reaches the backend LLM. The system is trained via supervised fine-tuning on simulated injection attacks and uses both the original user instruction and the incoming data to identify and remove adversarial content while preserving legitimate information. Evaluation across multiple benchmarks demonstrates that DataFilter reduces prompt injection attack success rates to near zero while maintaining the functional utility of the underlying LLM, offering a practical drop-in defense solution.
connects: This work addresses prompt injection attacks, a critical vulnerability class that emerged as LLMs became integrated into autonomous agent systems that interact with untrusted external data sources. The paper situates itself against existing defense approaches: fine-tuning methods that require model access, detection-based defenses that suffer high false-positive rates and utility degradation, and system-level defenses requiring architectural redesign. DataFilter builds on the broader literature of input sanitization and data filtering techniques, adapting supervised learning approaches to the security domain. It also connects to research on adversarial robustness in NLP and the study of how instructions propagate through multi-component LLM systems, positioning data-layer defense as a complementary approach to model-centric security measures.
enables: DataFilter establishes the data layer as a promising defense frontier for LLM security, potentially motivating further research into input sanitization for language models. The model-agnostic design suggests a pathway toward composable security layers that can protect diverse LLM deployments without retraining. Future work could extend this approach to more sophisticated injection attacks, explore the robustness of the filter itself against adaptive adversaries, and investigate how to integrate DataFilter with other defensive strategies. The work also raises questions about optimal placement of defenses in LLM agent architectures, whether multiple filtering stages improve robustness, and how to scale these approaches to handle diverse data modalities and domain-specific injection patterns in real-world deployments.
